name: Wolfe et al. Simulated Essay Quality Evaluation Extract
population_or_context: Student essay responses evaluated for data-augmentation suitability in automated scoring.
sample_size: 96 real student essays and 288 simulated essays generated from three prompt-engineering strategies.
methods: Extracted expert scoring, agreement analysis, score-distribution comparison, and realism-rating methods for evaluating LLM-simulated essays.
education_context: Student essay responses scored with a four-point rubric; grade band and prompt family are study-specific.
response_origin: Large-language-model simulated essays are generated from real student essays and evaluated against the real essay score distribution and expert judgments.
label_source: Expert raters assign scores and realism judgments to simulated essays; original real essays have professional ratings.
generation_model: Large language model; the public preprint text reviewed here does not name a specific model version.
generation_method: Three prompt-based methods generate simulated essays from real student essays.
prompt_strategy: 25 Examples, Predict Next, and Sentence prompting strategies are compared.
evaluation_method: Human scoring agreement between original and simulated essays, score-distribution comparisons, and expert realism ratings.
scorer_use: Calibration and data-augmentation suitability for automated scoring; not direct validation of a synthetic-only scorer test set.
real_response_comparator: Real student essays are the comparator for simulated essay quality and score preservation.
reported_metrics: Study reports exact and adjacent agreement, weighted kappa, score-distribution differences, and realism ratings across prompting methods.
limitations: The paper is a preprint focused on generated-data quality for augmentation.
Expert realism and score preservation do not by themselves establish downstream automated-scorer validity.
The quality evidence is essay-specific and prompt-strategy-specific.
